elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we first introduce and study the notions of $S$-pure exact sequences and $S$-absolutely pure modules which extend the classical notions of pure exact sequences and absolutely pure modules.
elements.sent-2411-002: And then, we give some new characterizations of $S$-Noetherian rings and $S$-coherent rings in terms of $S$-absolutely pure modules.
elements.sent-2411-003: Throughout this paper, $R$ is always a commutative ring with identity, all modules are unitary and $S$ is always a multiplicative subset of $R$, that is, $1\in S$ and $s_1s_2\in S$ for any $s_1\in S, s_2\in S$.
elements.sent-2411-004: In 2002, Anderson and Dumitrescu \cite{ad02 introduced $S$-Noetherian rings $R$ in which every ideal $I$ of $R$ is $S$-finite, that is, there exists a finitely generated sub-ideal $K$ of $I$ such that $sI\subseteq K$.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Cohen's Theorem, Eakin-Nagata Theorem and Hilbert Basis Theorem for $S$-Noetherian rings are given in \cite{ad02 .}
elements.sent-2411-006: However, there is no module-theoretic characterizations of $S$-Noetherian rings until recently.
elements.sent-2411-007: The author in \cite{zmcswnoem shows that a ring $R$ is $S$-Noetherian if and only if any direct sums (or, direct limits) of injective $R$-modules is $S$-injective (see \cite[Theorem 3.4]{zmcswnoem ).}}
elements.sent-2411-008: It is well-known that a ring $R$ is a Noetherian ring if and only if every absolutely pure $R$-module is injective (see \cite[Theorem 3]{M70 ).}
elements.sent-2411-009: A natural question is that Question A: How to characterize $S$-Noetherian rings in terms of some $S$-version of absolutely pure modules? Actually, in this paper we show that a ring $R$ is $S$-Noetherian if and only any $S$-absolutely pure (or, absolutely pure ) module is $S$-injective (see Theorem \ref{usnoe-s-abp ).}
elements.sent-2411-010: In 2018, Bennis and Hajoui \cite{bh18 introduced the notions of $S$-finitely presented modules and $S$-coherent modules which can be seen as $S$-versions of finitely presented modules and coherent modules.}
elements.sent-2411-011: An $R$-module $M$ is said to be $S$-finitely presented provided there exists an exact sequence of $R$-modules \begin{equation}0 \rightarrow K\rightarrow F\rightarrow M\rightarrow 0,\end{equation} where $K$ is $S$-finite and $F$ is finitely generated free.
elements.sent-2411-012: An $R$-module $M$ is said to be $S$-coherent if it is finitely generated and every finitely generated submodule of $M$ is $S$-finitely presented.
elements.sent-2411-013: A ring $R$ is called an $S$-coherent ring if $R$ itself is an $S$-coherent $R$-module, that is, every finitely generated ideal of $R$ is $S$-finitely presented.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, the author, Qi and Zhao \cite{qwz23 gave an $S$-version of Chase Theorem in terms of $S$-flat modules, i.e., modules $M$ satisfying $M_S$ is a flat $R_S$-module.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Then the following assertions are equivalent:.
elements.sent-2411-016: It is well-known that a ring $R$ is a coherent ring if and only if every pure quotient of an absolutely pure module is absolutely pure, if and only if every direct limits of absolutely pure modules is absolutely pure (see \cite[Theorem 3.2]{S70 ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: A natural question is that Question B: How to characterize $S$-coherent rings in terms of some $S$-version of absolutely pure modules? Actually, we show that a ring $R$ is an $S$-coherent ring if and only if every ($S$-)pure quotient of an ($S$-)absolutely pure module is $S$-absolutely pure, if and only if every direct limits of absolutely pure modules is $S$-absolutely pure (see Theorem \ref{s-d-d-non ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recall from \cite{zwz21 that an $R$-module $T$ is called a $u$-$S$-torsion $($abbreviates uniformly $S$-torsion$)$ module provided that there exists an element $s\in S$ such that $sT=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: An $R$-sequence $M\xrightarrow{f N\xrightarrow{g L$is said to be $u$-$S$-exact (at $N$) provided that there is an element $s\in S$ such that $s\Ker(g)\subseteq \Im(f)$ and $s\Im(f)\subseteq \Ker(g)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: A long $R$-sequence is said to be $u$-$S$-exact if it $u$-$S$-exact at any place.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recall from \cite{R79 that an exact sequence $0\rightarrow A\rightarrow B\rightarrow C\rightarrow 0$ is said to be pure provided that for any finitely presented $R$-module $M$, the induced sequence $0\rightarrow M\otimes_RA\rightarrow M\otimes_RB\rightarrow M\otimes_RC\rightarrow 0$ is also exact.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Now we introduce the uniformly $S$-version of pure exact sequences.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $R$ be a ring and $S$ a multiplicative subset of $R$.
elements.sent-2411-024: A short exact sequence $0\rightarrow A\rightarrow B\rightarrow C\rightarrow 0$ is said to be $S$-pure provided that for any finitely presented $R$-module $M$, the induced sequence \begin{equation}0\rightarrow M\otimes_RA\rightarrow M\otimes_RB\rightarrow M\otimes_RC\rightarrow 0\end{equation} is $u$-$S$-exact.
elements.sent-2411-025: Obviously, any pure exact sequence is $S$-pure.
elements.sent-2411-026: In \cite[34.5]{w , there are many characterizations of pure exact sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The next result generalizes some of these characterizations to $S$-pure exact sequences.

elements.sent-2411-183: In this paper, we first introduce and study the notions of $s$-pure exact sequences and $s$-absolutely pure modules which extend the classical notions of pure exact sequences and absolutely pure modules.
elements.sent-2411-184: And then, we give some new characterizations of $S$-Noetherian rings and $S$-coherent rings in terms of $s$-absolutely pure modules.
elements.sent-2411-185: The author in \cite{zmcswnoem shows that a ring $R$ is $S$-Noetherian if and only if any direct sums (or, direct limits) of injective $R$-modules is $s$-injective (see \cite[Theorem 3.4]{zmcswnoem ).}}
elements.sent-2411-186: A natural question is that Question A: How to characterize $S$-Noetherian rings in terms of some $S$-version of absolutely pure modules? Actually, in this paper we show that a ring $R$ is $S$-Noetherian if and only any $s$-absolutely pure (or, absolutely pure ) module is $s$-injective (see Theorem \ref{usnoe-s-abp ).}
elements.sent-2411-187: We give a new $S$-version of Chase Theorem in this paper (see Theorem \ref{newchase ).}
elements.sent-2411-188: It is also well-known that a ring $R$ is coherent if and only if $\Hom_R(E,I)$ is flat for any absolutely pure module $E$ and injective module $I$, if and only if $\Hom_R(\Hom_R(F,I_1),I_2)$ is flat for any flat module $F$ and any injective modules $I_1$ and $I_2$ (see \cite[Lemma 4]{CD93 ).}
elements.sent-2411-189: A natural question is that Question B: How to characterize $S$-coherent rings in terms of some $S$-version of absolutely pure modules? Actually, we show that a ring $R$ is an $S$-coherent ring if and only if every ($s$-)pure quotient of an ($s$-)absolutely pure module is $s$-absolutely pure, if and only if every direct limits of absolutely pure modules is $s$-absolutely pure (see Theorem \ref{s-d-d-non ).}
elements.sent-2411-190: We also show that a ring $R$ is an $S$-coherent ring if and only if $\Hom_R(E,I)$ is $s$-flat for any $s$-absolutely pure module $E$ and any injective module $I$, if and only if if $I$ is an injective cogenerator, then $\Hom_R(E,I)$ is $s$-flat for any $s$-absolutely pure module $E$, if and only if $\Hom_R(\Hom_R(F,I_1),I_2)$ is $s$-flat for any $s$-flat module $F$ and any injective modules $I_1$ and $I_2$, if and only if if $I_1$ and $I_2$ are injective cogenerators, then $\Hom_R(\Hom_R(F,I_1),I_2)$ is $s$-flat for any $s$-flat module $F$ (see Theorem \ref{phi-coh-fp ).}
elements.sent-2411-191: An $R$-homomorphism $M\xrightarrow{f N$ is called a $u$-$S$-monomorphism (resp., $u$-$S$-epimorphism, $u$-$S$-isomorphism) if $0\rightarrow M\xrightarrow{f N$ (resp., $M\xrightarrow{f N\rightarrow 0$, $0\rightarrow M\xrightarrow{f N\rightarrow 0$) is $u$-$S$-exact.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-288: And then, we give some new characterizations of $S$-coherent rings in terms of $s$-absolutely pure modules.
elements.sent-2411-289: A natural question is that Question: How to characterize $S$-coherent rings in terms of a certain $S$-version of absolutely pure modules? Actually, we show that a ring $R$ is an $S$-coherent ring if and only if every ($s$-)pure quotient of an ($s$-)absolutely pure module is $s$-absolutely pure, if and only if every direct limits of absolutely pure modules is $s$-absolutely pure (see Theorem \ref{s-d-d-non ).}
elements.sent-2411-290: The next result generalizes some of those characterizations to $s$-pure exact sequences.
elements.sent-2411-291: Recall thatAn $R$-module $M$ is said to be absolutely pure provided that $\Ext_R^1(N,M)=0$ for any finitely presented $R$-module $N$.
